relationship_to_restored_text: Interpretive contemplative layer aligned by verse. This does not replace the source-audited restored translation.

verses.mystical_translation: Arad attacks and takes captives from Israel.
Israel answers with a vow of total devotion against the cities.
The ancient war narrative records victory and destruction at Hormah.
Israel goes around Edom, and the road wears down the people's spirit.
Exhaustion turns into accusation against Divine provision.
Fiery serpents enter the camp, and many die.
The people confess, and Moses intercedes again.
A serpent image is raised as a sign toward life.
The bitten look toward the bronze serpent and live.
The journey resumes at Oboth.
The camp moves toward Moab's eastern wilderness.
They rest at wadi Zered.
The Arnon marks a border between peoples.
An ancient book of wars preserves fragments of the route.
The poem remembers valleys leaning toward Moab.
At Beer, YHWH promises water to the gathered people.
Israel sings for the well to rise.
Leaders and nobles dig with staffs and authority turned toward provision.
The stations carry the people onward.
Pisgah opens a view across the wasteland.
Israel asks Sihon for passage.
They promise to remain on the road and avoid fields, vineyards, and wells.
Sihon refuses and comes out for battle.
Israel defeats him and takes the territory.
Israel settles in the Amorite cities.
Heshbon already carries a prior history of conquest over Moab.
The proverb-makers celebrate the city of Sihon.
War poetry imagines fire consuming Moab's strongholds.
The poem mourns Moab's sons and daughters under captivity.
The victory song expands the devastation across the land.
Israel dwells in Amorite territory.
Jazer and its settlements are taken.
Og of Bashan comes out for battle at Edrei.
Moses is told not to fear and is promised victory.
The chapter closes with totalizing victory and possession language that must remain under ethical critique.

contemplative_note: Numbers 21 moves between war, exhaustion, healing, travel, song, water, borders, and conquest. Its healing images must not erase the violence around them, and its war language must never become permission for modern territorial or religious destruction.
